attestation.scope: faithfulness of the transmission (script) layer to the Popol Wuj

beats.title: The mud person
beats.script: The creators fashion a being from mud and earth. It speaks, but its face is lopsided; it cannot turn its head; it cannot look behind it. When it gets wet, it softens and dissolves. It has no mind of its own. They let it dissolve back into the ground.
beats.application: The first attempt at a genuine making falls apart under the first real moisture — the first rain, the first test, the first real encounter with the world. It could make sounds but could not turn to face what was behind it. Every generation produces its mud people: forms of selfhood that perform speech but cannot hold their shape when the weather comes. The honest response is to let them dissolve and begin again with better substance.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The wooden people
beats.script: The creators carve the people from wood. These ones walk, talk, multiply, and populate the face of the earth. But they have no sweat, no moisture, no blood. Their faces are drawn and yellow. Their flesh is dry. And most gravely: they have no memory of their makers, no acknowledgment of the Heart of Sky and Heart of Earth. They just walk around.
beats.application: The wooden people are the most dangerous counterfeit because they are the most convincing. They do everything a person does — they move, they speak, they multiply, they fill the earth. The single thing they cannot do is remember who made them and turn toward the source. An age of wooden people is an age of forms that function without gratitude, production without acknowledgment, speech without prayer. No sweat: they have never been in the fire. No blood: nothing has ever cost them anything.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The flood and the animals turn
beats.script: A great rain of resin falls from the sky. The animals they kept but never cared for turn on them first — the deer, the birds, all the creatures they hunted and used. 'You caused us pain. You ate us. Now we will eat you,' they say, and they do.
beats.application: The accounting begins with the animals, with the ones you kept but did not honor. Every system that extracts without acknowledgment — from the land, from the creatures, from the people it treats as its instruments — eventually faces this turn. The animals do not rebel out of malice; they speak the plain truth of what was done to them. The unacknowledged always have the first word when the reckoning comes.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The uprising of the tools
beats.script: Their own possessions rise against them. The grinding stone speaks: 'We were ground upon. Every day, every day at night, at dawn, for your sake we went hurl hurl, and now you will feel our force.' The griddles, plates, and cooking pots speak. The dogs speak: 'You gave us nothing, you drove us away, you preferred the ones who would bite us.' Everything they used without acknowledgment finds its voice and uses them in return.
beats.application: The grinding stone's complaint is exact: every day, every day, at night, at dawn — the labor that was invisible is named aloud. The wooden people's tools know their hours of use down to the detail. This is the uprising of unacknowledged labor, the revolt of infrastructure, the day when the unseen instruments of daily life speak their own names. The maize we grind, the earth we turn, the workers whose names we do not know — every one of them is keeping its own precise count.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The wood people become monkeys
beats.script: The wooden people who survive the uprising flee into the forests and climb the trees. Their faces shrink; their bodies change. They become the monkeys that live in the trees today. This is why monkeys look so like people: they are the people who came before, the ones who could not remember. They are a sign left in the world of what personhood looks like when it loses its source.
beats.application: The monkey in the tree is the human being who could not acknowledge its making. It still has the face, still has the hands, still makes the sounds — but it has lost the road. This is why Jun B'atz' and Jun Chowen, the artisan-brothers who would not honor the emerging creative principle, also became monkeys: the Popol Wuj is drawing the same portrait twice. Talent, skill, and human form are not enough to make a person. Memory of the source is the one thing that cannot be imitated, performed, or manufactured from wood.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The animals show the way to Paxil and Cayala
beats.script: After the destruction, the creators seek the right substance. It is not the great powers who find it. The mountain cat, the coyote, the parrot, and the crow know the way. They lead to Paxil and Cayala, the great mountain of food, where the yellow and white ears of maize grow along with cacao, sapodilla, and countless fruits. The animals found it because they have always been close to the earth.
beats.application: The knowledge of what we are actually made of does not come from the great creative powers working in their councils. It comes through the small ones — the ones closer to the ground, the ones the wooden people used and did not thank. The mountain cat, the coyote, the parrot, the crow are not impressive guides, but they know where the food is. The substance of genuine human making is found by following the ones the previous age treated as instruments.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The white and yellow maize
beats.script: Inside the mountain of Paxil and Cayala grow the white ears and the yellow ears of maize. These are the ones — not mud, not wood, not stone — that carry the right substance. From these will come the flesh, the fat, and the blood of the people who will remember.
beats.application: Every failed creation tried a harder material — mud, then wood, then stone was attempted — when the right substance was always the softest and most perishable: grain. What we are made of is not durable in itself; it must be tended, planted, grown season by season, ground by hand. The substance of a remembering person is not stone. It is something that requires the labor of the grandmother's hands every time it is needed.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: IxMuqane grinds nine times
beats.script: IxMuqane takes the white and yellow maize. She grinds it nine times. From the grinding come the fat and the flesh; from it come the blood. With water she wets her hands and works the dough. This is the substance from which the first true human beings will be formed.
beats.application: IxMuqane is the axis of the whole trilogy. She read the tz'ite seeds to divine the fate of the descent; she tended the Twin-heads of maize that were the life-sign of the Hero Twins in Xibalba; now she grinds those shoots' kin into the substance of humanity — nine patient passes of the stone, the same stone the Lords of Death used to mill the Twins. What was the death-mill of Xibalba is the genesis-mill of the people. The same hands that tended the green shoots watching for signs of life below are the hands that grind us into being. Nine times: the making of a person is not a single act. It requires patient, skilled repetition. It requires a grandmother who has not stopped caring.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The four first humans
beats.script: From the dough are formed the four first men: B'alam Kiche', B'alam Aq'ab', Majukutaj, and Iq'i B'alam. They are beautiful, perfectly formed, and wise. They have blood, they have sweat, they have moisture. They can see everything — everything in the sky, everything on the face of the earth — and they give thanks immediately, turning toward their makers.
beats.application: The first act of a properly made human being is gratitude. Not self-appraisal, not celebration of their own beauty, not an account of their capacities. They turn toward the source and give thanks. This is the exact thing the wooden people could never do — and it is the first thing, before anything else, that the maize people do with their perfect new sight.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: They see too much
beats.script: But their sight is too complete. They can see to the ends of the sky and earth in all four directions. They know everything. 'It is not good,' say the Heart of Sky and the Plumed Serpent. 'They will become as we are — gods — if they can see everything and know everything. They will have nowhere left to go.' A being who sees everything is already finished.
beats.application: Total clarity is the end of growth. The instrument that claims to see everything, to know everything, to have mapped the whole terrain — has nowhere left to go, no more to reach for, nothing left to discover. It has made itself complete, which is the same as making itself finished. This is the lesson Wuqub Kak'ix refused: the false sun insisted on its own totality and had its jewels pulled out. The maize people have genuinely achieved what Wuqub Kak'ix only pretended to — and it is immediately recognized as a problem, not a triumph. Seeing everything is not the goal of human making.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The smoke veil — sight dimmed as gift
beats.script: Heart of Sky breathes mist over their eyes, as one breathes on a mirror. Their sight is dimmed — not taken away, dimmed. They can see clearly nearby but not to the farthest distances. They are made as they ought to be: able to see well enough to live, but not so completely that living becomes pointless. This is how they remain human and do not become something else.
beats.application: The mist breathed on the mirror is a gift, not a punishment. To be a creature who cannot see everything — who must ask, must seek, must stay curious, must look again tomorrow — is to be properly alive. The one who sees everything has consumed the future. The one who sees only nearby has somewhere still to go, something still to learn, someone still worth asking. Incompleteness is the condition of genuine human flourishing. The smoke veil is the shape of that condition, breathed onto us with care by the one who made us, so that we would remain the kind of thing that keeps entering the word.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: Made while the four sleep
beats.script: While the four first men sleep, the Heart of Sky makes four women for them: Kaqa Paluma, Chomija, Tzununija, and Kaquixaja. They are beautiful and perfectly formed. When the men wake and find them there, they are made complete.
beats.application: What completes us arrives while we are not watching for it. The pairing is not achieved by the one who is paired; it is given during the not-seeing, during the sleep, during the surrender of the drive to control the shape of one's own completion. The four first mothers are not made by the men, not summoned by them, not designed. They arrive in the dark while someone else is tending the making.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The long vigil in darkness
beats.script: The four couples wait in the darkness before the first sunrise. They came from the east, from where the dawn would rise, but it has not come. They pray; they burn copal resin; they weep; they keep their faces turned toward the place where the light will appear. The waiting in the dark is long.
beats.application: Between the making and the sunrise there is always a long vigil. The properly made human being is not immediately lit — it waits in the dark, praying, burning what it has, oriented toward the direction of the light it has not yet seen. The ones who greet the first dawn are the ones who kept their faces turned east before there was any reason to. Orientation in darkness precedes illumination. The burning copal in the dark is the same smoke that blinded the Lords of Xibalba: what consecrates also protects.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The first sunrise
beats.script: The sun rises for the first time. The animals of the mountains and the flatlands cry out together. The first four men and their wives weep with joy; they burn their offerings at once. The Plumed Serpent and Heart of Sky are there, having been present at every step of the making. The light arrives, and the world is oriented.
beats.application: The first true sunrise is the completion of the whole trilogy. The false sun was cleared away; the descent was survived; the proper human being was made and dimmed and paired and kept vigil — and now the real light comes. Its arrival is not triumph; it is tears. The ones who have waited longest, who have faced east in the dark and burned what they had, who have been ground and sown and risen — when the light finally comes they do not shout. They weep. That weeping is the right response to the real sunrise, the one that cost everything before it came.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)

beats.title: The first prayer — acknowledgment of the making
beats.script: The four men speak the first prayer. They name the Heart of Sky, the Heart of Earth, the Maker and the Modeler. 'Heart of Sky, Heart of Earth, give us our sign, our word, as long as there is day, as long as there is light. You who speak of us, who think of us — hear us. Do not let us fall, do not let us be destroyed.' The first fully formed speech of the maize people is the acknowledgment of the source.
beats.application: The difference between the wooden people and the maize people is this prayer. The wood could speak — it spoke volubly, it filled the earth with sound — but it could not turn toward the source and name it. The maize people's first complete act of consciousness is not self-expression. It is acknowledgment: you who think of us, who speak of us, hear us. This is the human vocation the trilogy has been building toward. The false sun could not acknowledge anything above itself. The wooden people could not acknowledge anything at all. The maize people, ground and formed and dimmed and kept in the dark, rise at the first light and turn their voices toward the source of their making. That turning is what the word has always been for: Katok pa tzij — enter the word — and the word carries you back to the Heart of Sky, Heart of Earth, the Maker and the Modeler who thought of us first.

provenance.source: paraphrase of published Popol Wuj translations (Christenson; Tedlock)
